segments.text: Hi everybody. My uh my name is Olivier
Gdan. I'm the co-founder of Sonar and
today I'm going to talk about the newly
released capability of architecture in
Sonar Cube. Um this presentation is
going to be divided into three parts. Uh
first I'm going to talk about the
problem we are trying to solve. Second
part is going to be about the solution
what we have implemented in in Sona to
solve this and the third part is going
to be what's uh what's next for you. So
let me start with the problem. Um so
basically as you know the world operates
on software which means that uh software
is now uh the single most valuable asset
for organization and one thing which
reduces um this value is actually
technical debt which I think we have in
the last 17 years done a very good job
to to help organization um handle u but
we have mostly worked at the code level
which is we have helped people to work
on their maintenability, reliability on
security
um with Sonar Cube and we felt it was
time to actually move into more like
structural debt which is about how the
um how the software how the application
is organized.
What's what are the abstractions? What's
the design? Do uh components have really
uh single responsibility
etc etc.
And when you do not follow this, it's
actually not that much fun, which is uh
you can see symptoms like um engineers
are leaving because it's uh the
environment is too messy. Every change
you make has side effects and which are
very difficult to explain and overall uh
your velocity is going to um uh to drop.
So the these are the symptoms of not
managing architecture. What's also um
what you should also know is that it
doesn't come like uh suddenly. This is
very um uh sneaky the way the way it uh
arises and it's it's very much an
erosion which is generally at software
creation application creation it's there
is no real problem. People thought about
what they want to build. They organize
it well.
And it's okay. But little by little uh
when you make uh shortcuts during
development to save time or because you
don't feel ready to do a bigger
investment uh it start to introduce that
uh erosion and you start to see the pain
and generally when you start to feel the
pain uh it's too late and uh in order to
change things it's going to require a
major change. So not really easy to uh
to deal with
and the goal here of a good architecture
is really that it should be easily
understood by everybody. It should be
modular and having that health in your
architecture will really be the only way
you can maintain um long-term speed
long-term velocity in your in your
development. So this is really the
problem we are uh trying to solve with
this new capability in sonar cube.
So now um solution um I'm going to go
through the solution philosophically but
also in terms of uh concept. So first
let me start a little bit with concept.
So what are we um what what's
architecture and architecture we divide
it into three. One is what is the
structure of your application which is
what are the components and how are they
organized in other words what's a
hierarchy of components. That's number
one. Number two is
what are the relations between the
component which is who
um has a relation what component has a
relation with what component
and the third one is really about design
which is hey how can a component uh how
should a component be used by other
components and once you put these three
things together and you start to manage
them together you actually manage your
architecture
So what we are doing um in sonar cube is
we have introduced um three concepts.
One is the current architecture and the
current architecture is what you what is
the actual architecture which is what do
you have today and we actually compute
this um at every analysis of your code.
So we reverse engineer and we present
you with a map of your current
architecture. I'm I'm going to go um
into this in more details uh in a
minute. The second thing the second
concept is really the intended
architecture which is what are you
shooting for? Sometimes it's going to be
like what did we intend to do initially
sometimes is what do we intend to do
now? It doesn't really matter where this
is coming from. This is really what you
are trying to achieve. And then the next
thing is about what we call deviations
which is this is really the difference
between the current architecture and the
intended architecture
and this is a basis for uh our solution.
So our philosophy from a product point
of view is that we are not we have not
built a um a product for architect. We
actually have built a product for team
which is and team
actually actually include architect but
this is not it. So it's about making
sure people understand what they are
dealing with what kind of animal they
are dealing with with their application
that they understand how it's structured
how what are the relationships etc etc.
you can force it and al it's about also
remediation which is what we have tried
to do is to really make things um
actionable
and we have made it so that human will
decide and then
um
uh stakeholders uh will solve the
problem which is stakeholder can be
human can be AI agent it doesn't really
matter but human will decide um for for
the for the application. So this is our
philosophy for that uh new capability
and the solution is built really on four
uh pillars. One is um we want everyone
so human or AI agents to be able to
understand the current architecture. So
everybody should have access as as part
of the let's say like a live
documentation should have access to the
current um architecture so that they
understand what's happening and we make
sure they actually follow um what what
is there. The second thing is ability to
formalize intended architecture and here
we I think we have done a good job in
making it very simple to understand
what's there. Um the way it is
formalized is very simple to understand
but it's also very easy to
define and update um create and update
uh this intended architecture which is
our approach makes it extremely simple
to um to
define it the first time and to
incrementally uh improve it in the
future.
The third thing is we have a clear way
of prioritizing issues which means um
one issue defined somewhere will not uh
overlap with another issue. For example,
as soon as we find um as as soon as we
find that there is a structural issue,
we actually ignore all um subsequent
relation uh issues that that are due to
that structural issue. So we only report
issues once and finally we make this
extremely uh actionable thanks to sonar
cube as we leverage all the capabilities
there workflow quality gate uh showing
issue in the code etc to make it really
actionable when it comes to remediation.
So let me uh dig a little bit into
current architecture. So the goal here
is really to discover slash explore
to understand the current architecture.
So here we have a full capability to see
top level up to be able to zoom in um um
obviously zoom out see the relations
understand the
um the complexity of the relations to
understand also the complexity of
components to understand whether they
are highly coupled um whether they are
uh they have a low uh coupling and to
understand what are the key components
not um you will see when you explore
your application that um certain
components they are super key and should
you have to change them uh it would be
extremely difficult. So that's really
the goal of our current architecture. Um
then the intended architecture is really
about defining what do you want to put
under control. Uh intended architecture
should not map your entire app. uh it
doesn't make sense if you if you start
to map your entire app. Um it means that
you're going to have pretty much to
change it every time you you you change
the app. And this is not what you want.
What you want there is to decide what's
important uh really starting from the
the top level. What's important? what do
you want to put under control
um in terms of components and then you
define the relationship between these
important components
and then it becomes really the blueprint
for all stakeholders. So human agent
doesn't matter this will be uh their
reference and every time um they drift
from that internet architecture the um
every time they will actually get
warnings it will break quality gate etc
etc
and how do you define this intended
architecture we have actually put a few
constraints in place in order to first
simplify the way you define architecture
second is it enables to easily
edit what you have. So be incremental.
And thirdly, it helps to make sure that
if three pe three different people want
to define the same architecture, the the
definition will be exactly identical. So
there are not several ways to do the
same thing. So how do we do it? do this
first. Um, you start top down which is
you cannot just work on a specific part
of the app. You have to really uh define
from the top um uh the the part you want
to work on. Um the second thing is which
is super important only what you add
will be verified which is anything which
is not in your intended architecture
will actually be ignored when we run um
the analysis. The third thing is that
you can only define a load dependencies
between components. You cannot say this
compon this uh relationship is
forbidden. Actually the way to say this
is to not define a dependency.
The fourth thing also it's in the third
bullet point here is that this allow
dependency will only be defined between
siblings I components that have the same
part which is you cannot define any
other type of dependencies and
dependencies will be inherit uh
inherited uh by sub components which
mean if you define for example in Java
that a relation is a load between module
A and module B it means that every
subcomponent of module A can um have a
relation with every uh component in
module B. For now we h we are not
dealing with design. Uh this is coming
in the next version and once we deal
with design we will also be able to say
um when you access module B you can only
access it through XYZ um which are
submponents of um module B. And finally
um dependencies are not transitive which
means if module A can have a relation to
module B and module B can have a
relation to module C it doesn't mean
module A can have a relation with module
C uh actually the opposite which is
unless you define specifically module A
to module C dependency it will be
forbidden. So these are the rules um
that you you have to use to create the
internet architecture which will make
that the uh it will be uniquely defined.
It will be unique once you define it. So
now let's talk a little bit about the
workflow here. So I mentioned earlier
um that as soon as you run an analysis
you get access to current architecture.
um you then can define your intended
architecture and once you have both we
are able to generate um what we call
architecture issues. Architecture issues
are actually divided into two. one is
deviations
which I mentioned earlier which is
really the difference between current
architecture and intended architecture
and the second type of issues that we
generate are what we call architecture
flows which is these issues are generic
they don't really depend upon your
intended architecture uh these are
things like tongles which is like uh
cycle dependencies between uh component
and sometimes
Tongle cycle, tongled cycle,
dependencies between components,
oversized component, uh too much
coupling, low coupling, etc., etc. So,
all of this uh comes as part of what we
call the architecture flow. So
um flows plus deviation make
architectural issues and these issues
get actually converted into what we call
sonar cube issues which are code level
issues which then becomes what you need
to solve in order to resolve the
architecture issues. Those sonar cube
issues they are very um they are very
useful because they actually show up in
uh existing dashboard. they will break
uh quality gate uh in the future. They
will be shown in um in the ID also so
that it can be uh really um done uh
really on the uh on the left of your
process uh to to solve it or to not
introduce problems.
Once uh all of this happens um you you
will note that every um architecture
issue will actually appear uh in two
forms in two shapes. One is architecture
issues which is really the high level
issue which is okay module A should not
have a relationship to module B will be
an architectural issue for example and
then we are going to um transform this
to code issue which is this module A to
module B actually can be could be broken
like 30 times. If that's the case, we
will create 30 code issues which will
really enable you to track uh what's the
real uh depth that you have here and
also enable you uh to track the progress
when you remediate which is instead of
saying it's one issue and it's going to
take you weeks to solve. What you can
what you can see here it's it's actually
30 uh 30 code issues and every time you
fix one you're going to see the
progress. Finally I would like to um say
a word about some other work that we are
doing around architecture which is we
also are working on architecture as
context for AI agents and this comes as
um two twofold. One is um we are working
on providing the current architecture um
to AI agents so that they actually
deliver
code that works let's say they deliver
more often code that will work better uh
so they do a better job basically and
code that also to deliver that code uh
AI agents will also be more effective uh
I mean I think here you can do
comparison with human, which is if
tomorrow you have to work on an app that
you don't know and you have no idea how
it's constructed, um it's going to take
you a while to figure that out and the
uh the probability that you actually do
a good job is lower than if you fully
know the architecture. Same for agents.
Here we want to provide this current
architecture as context for agents. The
second part is we want to also provide
intended architecture as context for
agent in order for agents to deliver
something that compliance to
what we want them to do which is
basically the blueprint uh for
architecture. So we are working on these
two things which should be delivered um
pretty soon.
Finally, I would like to talk to to talk
about on now what um which is what's
what's next for you uh if you want to
engage with this new capability. So um
for now architecture is only available
in sonarcube cloud. Um it comes as part
of all plans there and uh it comes at no
additional cost. Uh we are planning to
uh release son server later on this
year. Uh it is available for five uh
main languages. Java, JavaScript,
TypeScript, Python and C. Um again we
are we are going to add more languages
later on this year. Um once you activate
the feature uh you will get um
architecture tab which is uh uh the
entry point for architecture. It's a new
dashboard and you should know that an
analysis is required for when you make
any change. So for any change to be
taken into account uh you will need to
run an analysis. Right now we have about
800,000 uh lock limit uh to make sure we
have good performances and that's about
it. And just would like to um mention
that everything I've described here will
be demoed by Christian J in um the next
session. So you will really have the
sonar cube architecture in action um in
that other session. Thank you for your
attention.
